sources.title: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.2
supports: Normative success criteria and conformance requirements for WCAG 2.2
does_not_support: Claim that WCAG covers every disability need
Claim that automated tools alone establish conformance
limitations: Technology-specific implementation guidance is outside the normative core
Conformance still depends on scope, accessibility support and complete evaluation

sources.title: Understanding WCAG 2.2
supports: Intent, benefits, examples, sufficient techniques, advisory techniques and failures
limitations: Informative and updateable; not part of the normative standard

sources.title: Accessible Rich Internet Applications (WAI-ARIA) 1.2
sources.institution: W3C Accessible Rich Internet Applications Working Group
supports: Roles, states, properties and accessibility semantics for rich web applications
limitations: Semantic exposure does not guarantee usable behavior

sources.title: ARIA Authoring Practices Guide
sources.institution: W3C ARIA Authoring Practices Task Force
supports: Common widget semantics, keyboard interaction models, names, descriptions and landmark practices
does_not_support: Guarantee that every example is production-ready for every stack
limitations: APG explicitly is not a normative standard

sources.title: Evaluating Web Accessibility Overview
supports: Combination of tools and knowledgeable human review
limitations: Overview resource; detailed methods remain in linked documents

sources.title: Website Accessibility Conformance Evaluation Methodology (WCAG-EM)
supports: Scope definition, site exploration, representative sampling, evaluation and reporting
does_not_support: Universal applicability of draft WCAG-EM 2.0 as final standard
limitations: WCAG-EM 1.0 is a Working Group Note
WCAG-EM 2.0 remains draft as of the research date

sources.title: Accessibility Conformance Testing (ACT) Rules
supports: Transparent automated, semi-automated and manual test rules
does_not_support: Normative requirement to use ACT rules
limitations: Approved, proposed and deprecated statuses must be distinguished

sources.title: How People with Disabilities Use the Web
supports: Diverse auditory, cognitive, physical, speech and visual needs
Assistive technologies, temporary and situational limitations
does_not_support: Substitution for involvement of disabled users
limitations: Functional needs vary between individuals and contexts

sources.title: Involving Users in Evaluating Web Accessibility
supports: Combining standards evaluation with disabled-user involvement
does_not_support: Replacing standards-based evaluation with a small usability sample
limitations: User evaluation and conformance evaluation answer different questions
